Upper right and upper left abdominal regions, below the cartilage of the ribs. Right contains: liver, gallbladder, right kidney. Left contains: stomach, spleen, left kidney.

Central upper abdominal region, between the hypochondriac regions. Contains: stomach, liver (part), pancreas (part). Pain here can indicate gastritis, GERD, peptic ulcer, or cardiac event (referred pain).
Middle right and left abdominal regions (flank areas). Right contains: ascending colon, right kidney. Left contains: descending colon, left kidney. Flank pain often indicates kidney issues.
Central abdominal region surrounding the navel (umbilicus). Contains: small intestines, transverse colon. Umbilical pain can indicate appendicitis (early), intestinal obstruction, or hernia.
Lower right and left abdominal regions. Right iliac contains: appendix, cecum — RLQ pain = appendicitis (McBurney's point). Left iliac contains: sigmoid colon. Also site of inguinal hernia.
Central lower abdominal region below the umbilical region. Contains: urinary bladder, uterus (females), sigmoid colon. Pain here may indicate UTI, bladder infection, or reproductive organ issues.

Site of hematopoiesis (blood cell production). Produces RBCs, WBCs, and platelets. Found in spongy bone of sternum, ribs, vertebrae, pelvis, and ends of long bones. Yellow bone marrow stores fat and is found in shaft of long bones.

The lower and posterior part of the hip bone (os coxae). Part of the pelvic girdle. The ischial tuberosity ("sitting bone") bears weight when seated. The three parts of the hip bone: ilium (top), ischium (back-bottom), pubis (front).

term: the cellular components of blood include
definition: the cellular components of blood include

Red blood cell. Biconcave disc, no nucleus, lives ~120 days. Contains hemoglobin (Hgb) which carries oxygen. Normal count: males 4.5–5.5 million/µL; females 4.0–5.0 million/µL. Produced in red bone marrow. Destroyed by spleen/liver. Anemia = low RBC/Hgb.

term: the chambers of the heart are the
definition: the chambers of the heart are the

Lower two chambers of the heart. Right ventricle: pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs via pulmonary artery. Left ventricle: pumps oxygenated blood to body via aorta (thicker wall, higher pressure). Ventricular fibrillation = life-threatening arrhythmia requiring AED/defibrillation.

term: an organ located in the left upper quadrant is the
definition: an organ located in the left upper quadrant is the

Largest lymphoid organ, located in the left upper quadrant (LUQ). Functions: filters blood, destroys old/damaged RBCs, stores blood, immune response (produces lymphocytes/antibodies). Splenomegaly = enlarged spleen. Ruptured spleen = emergency.

term: the structure in the body also known as the voice box is the
definition: the structure in the body also known as the voice box is the

Voice box. Located between pharynx and trachea. Contains vocal cords (vocalis). Epiglottis covers larynx during swallowing to prevent aspiration. Laryngitis = inflammation. Laryngoscopy = visual exam.

term: the process that does not require oxygen for the breakdown of glucose is referre
definition: the process that does not require oxygen for the breakdown of glucose is referred to as being

Living or occurring without oxygen. Anaerobic bacteria cause infections in deep wounds (e.g., C. difficile, tetanus, gas gangrene). Anaerobic specimens must be collected without exposure to air and transported in special anaerobic transport media.

term: the funnel shaped basin that forms the upper end of the ureter is the
definition: the funnel shaped basin that forms the upper end of the ureter is the

Funnel-shaped structure at the center of the kidney that collects urine from the calyces and funnels it into the ureter. Pyelonephritis = infection of renal pelvis and kidney. Pyeloplasty = surgical repair.

term: in both males and females the entire pelvic floor is called the
definition: in both males and females the entire pelvic floor is called the

Diamond-shaped region between the thighs. In females: area between vaginal opening and anus. In males: area between scrotum and anus. Site of perineal lacerations during childbirth (episiotomy). Important for perineal care in catheter patients.

term: a pregnancy that develops in a location outside the uterine cavity is referred t
definition: a pregnancy that develops in a location outside the uterine cavity is referred to as

Fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, most commonly in the fallopian tube. Signs: sharp one-sided pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding, positive HCG. Medical emergency — can cause tube rupture and internal hemorrhage. Treated with methotrexate or surgery.

definition: which of the following body systems include the thyroid and pituitary glands
Circulatory

groin

term: a term that describes a solution that has the same concentration as cell fluids
definition: a term that describes a solution that has the same concentration as cell fluids is

Solution with same osmolarity as blood (0.9% normal saline, lactated Ringer's). Does not cause cells to shrink or swell. Hypotonic: less concentrated — cells swell. Hypertonic: more concentrated — cells shrink (crenation). Used in IV fluids.
The fibrous bands that support organs to hold them in place

term: the membrane that surrounds the heart is the
definition: the membrane that surrounds the heart is the

Double-layered fibrous sac surrounding the heart. Outer layer: fibrous pericardium (anchors heart). Inner layer: serous pericardium (visceral and parietal). Pericardial fluid lubricates heart movement. Pericarditis = inflammation; pericardial effusion = fluid buildup.

term: the small tip of cartilage at the lower end of the sternum is the
definition: the small tip of cartilage at the lower end of the sternum is the

Small cartilaginous extension at the inferior (bottom) tip of the sternum. Landmark for CPR hand placement (hands on lower half of sternum, ABOVE the xiphoid to avoid liver laceration). Ossifies in adulthood.

term: a major muscle in the body that assists in raising the arm away from the body is
definition: a major muscle in the body that assists in raising the arm away from the body is the

Large triangular muscle of the shoulder. Primary IM injection site in adults (deltoid IM: 1–3 inch, 23 gauge, 90°). Landmark: 2–3 finger widths below the acromion process. Can give 1–2 mL. Not used for infants/toddlers (underdeveloped).

term: cells that carry or transmit impulses toward the central nervous system are call
definition: cells that carry or transmit impulses toward the central nervous system are called

Carry nerve impulses FROM sensory receptors TO the brain/spinal cord. Mnemonic: Afferent = Arrive (to CNS). Efferent neurons = Exit (from CNS to muscles/glands). Interneurons connect afferent and efferent in the spinal cord.

term: the space in the brain where cerebrospinal fluid is form are the
definition: the space in the brain where cerebrospinal fluid is form are the

Lower two chambers of the heart. Right ventricle: pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs via pulmonary artery. Left ventricle: pumps oxygenated blood to body via aorta (thicker wall, higher pressure). Ventricular fibrillation = life-threatening arrhythmia requiring AED/defibrillation.

term: impulses from the receptors for smell are carried to the brain by the
definition: impulses from the receptors for smell are carried to the brain by the

Cranial nerve I. Responsible for sense of smell. Sensory fibers pass through the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone to the olfactory bulb. Loss of smell = anosmia (can occur with COVID-19, head trauma, or zinc deficiency).

term: the hormone that is essential for growth is produced in the
definition: the hormone that is essential for growth is produced in the

"Master gland" located at the base of the brain in the sella turcica. Anterior pituitary: secretes TSH, FSH, LH, ACTH, GH, prolactin. Posterior pituitary: releases ADH (antidiuretic hormone) and oxytocin. Controlled by hypothalamus.

term: what represent the layers of the heart
definition: what represent the layers of the heart
endocardium and myocardium

term: the blood vessel that brings blood from the head chest and arms back to the hear
definition: the blood vessel that brings blood from the head chest and arms back to the heart is the

Large vein that drains deoxygenated blood from the upper body (head, neck, arms, thorax) into the right atrium. Inferior vena cava (IVC) drains lower body. Together = venae cavae. SVC syndrome: compression causing facial swelling, seen in lung cancer.

term: what is the proper sequence for the flow of blood in the body
definition: what is the proper sequence for the flow of blood in the body
arteriole-capillary

term: the lymphatic system includes the following organs and tissue
definition: the lymphatic system includes the following organs and tissue
thymus spleen tonsils adenoids

term: The process in which white blood cells take in and destroy waste and foreign mat
definition: The process in which white blood cells take in and destroy waste and foreign material is called:

Process by which white blood cells (especially neutrophils and macrophages) engulf and destroy pathogens, dead cells, and debris. "Cell eating." Key immune defense. Neutrophils are first responders; macrophages are long-term phagocytes.

term: the structure common too the respiratory and digestive systems is the
definition: the structure common too the respiratory and digestive systems is the

Throat — muscular tube connecting nasal/oral cavities to larynx and esophagus. Three sections: nasopharynx (behind nose), oropharynx (behind mouth), laryngopharynx (connects to larynx/esophagus). Site of tonsils, adenoids. Pharyngitis = strep throat.

term: an accumulation of air in the pleural space that may lead to collapse of the lun
definition: an accumulation of air in the pleural space that may lead to collapse of the lungs is called

Air in the pleural space (between lung and chest wall) causing lung collapse. Types: spontaneous (tall thin individuals), traumatic (penetrating injury), tension (life-threatening — air accumulates under pressure). Signs: sudden chest pain, dyspnea, absent breath sounds. Emergency.

term: the leaf shaped cartilage that covers the opening of the larynx is the
definition: the leaf shaped cartilage that covers the opening of the larynx is the

Leaf-shaped cartilaginous flap that covers the larynx during swallowing to prevent food/liquid from entering the airway. Epiglottitis: life-threatening inflammation (bacterial, esp. H. influenzae type B) — "tripod position," drooling, stridor. Emergency.

term: most of the digestive process occurs in the
definition: most of the digestive process occurs in the

Primary site of nutrient absorption (~20 feet long). Three sections: duodenum (first 12 inches — receives bile/pancreatic enzymes), jejunum (middle), ileum (terminal — absorbs B12, bile salts). Villi increase surface area. Connects stomach to large intestine.
The lining of the stomach has many folds

term: the first portion of the small intestine is the
definition: the first portion of the small intestine is the

First and shortest section of the small intestine (~12 inches/"12 fingers" wide). Receives chyme from stomach, bile from common bile duct, and pancreatic enzymes. Site of most peptic ulcers. Duodenoscopy = visual examination with scope.
An abnormal accumulation of excessive fluid in the intercellular space is

definition: Hydronephrosis

term: the sex glands of the male and female reproductive system are the
definition: the sex glands of the male and female reproductive system are the

Primary reproductive organs. Testes (male): produce sperm and testosterone. Ovaries (female): produce eggs (ova) and estrogen/progesterone. Also function as endocrine glands. FSH stimulates follicle development in ovaries; LH triggers ovulation.

term: the bone that lives between the hip and the knee is the
definition: the bone that lives between the hip and the knee is the

Thigh bone — longest, strongest bone in the body. Connects hip (acetabulum) to knee (tibia/patella). Femoral artery runs medially — venipuncture landmark in emergencies. Femoral neck fractures common in elderly osteoporosis patients. Distal end has medial/lateral condyles.

term: the largest artery in the body is the
definition: the largest artery in the body is the

Section of the descending aorta below the diaphragm, supplying blood to abdominal organs. Bifurcates at L4 into left and right common iliac arteries. Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA): bulging — risk of rupture, medical emergency. Screened via ultrasound.

term: the term that refers to absence of menstrual flow
definition: the term that refers to absence of menstrual flow

Absence of menstrual flow. Primary: no period by age 16. Secondary: cessation after normal cycles for ≥3 months. Causes: pregnancy (first rule out), stress, extreme weight loss/gain, PCOS, thyroid disorders, hyperprolactinemia. A (without) + meno (menses) + rrhea (flow).
The hormone produced by the embryonic cells of the fetus
A substance necessary for proper formation of a blood clot

term: the left artioventricular (AV) valve in the heart is also known as the
definition: the left artioventricular (AV) valve in the heart is also known as the

Two-leaflet valve between left atrium and left ventricle. Prevents backflow during left ventricular contraction. Mitral valve prolapse (MVP): most common valve disorder. Mitral stenosis: narrowing. Mitral regurgitation: leaking. Heart sound S1 ("lub") = mitral and tricuspid closing.

term: the only artery in the body that carries
definition: the only artery in the body that carries

Only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. Exits the right ventricle, carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs for gas exchange. Pulmonary veins (4) carry oxygenated blood back to the left atrium — only veins carrying oxygenated blood.

term: a localized dilation resulting from weakness of a blood vessel wall is a
definition: a localized dilation resulting from weakness of a blood vessel wall is a

Abnormal bulging/weakening of an artery wall. Common sites: aorta (AAA), brain (berry aneurysm — causes subarachnoid hemorrhage if ruptured). Signs of rupture: sudden severe "thunderclap" headache (brain), severe abdominal/back pain (aorta). Medical emergency.
An example of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease

term: a portion of the large intestine is the
definition: a portion of the large intestine is the

Terminal section of the small intestine (last ~11 feet). Absorbs vitamin B12, bile salts, and remaining nutrients. Connects to large intestine at ileocecal valve. Crohn's disease commonly affects terminal ileum. Ileostomy: surgical opening of ileum to abdominal wall.
A chronic degenerative disease of the liver

term: the tube that permits urine to pass from the bladder to the outside of the body
definition: the tube that permits urine to pass from the bladder to the outside of the body is the

Tube carrying urine from bladder to outside. Female: ~4 cm (shorter — higher UTI risk). Male: ~20 cm (also carries semen). Urethritis: inflammation (often STI). Catheter inserted through urethra for urinary drainage. Urethral meatus = external opening.

term: the innermost layer of the uterus is the
definition: the innermost layer of the uterus is the

Inner lining of the uterus. Thickens under estrogen influence each cycle to prepare for implantation; sheds during menstruation (no pregnancy). Endometriosis: endometrial tissue outside uterus. Endometritis: inflammation. Endometrial cancer: most common GYN cancer.

The cause or origin of a disease or condition. Etiology of hypertension: often idiopathic (unknown); secondary causes include kidney disease, adrenal tumors. Knowing etiology guides treatment. Unknown etiology = idiopathic.

definition: the chronic skin condition characterized by a red, flat area covered with silvery scales is

Chronic autoimmune skin condition causing rapid skin cell turnover, resulting in thick, silvery-white scaly plaques on red patches. Commonly affects elbows, knees, scalp. Not contagious. Triggers: stress, infections, medications. Treated with topical steroids, biologics.

term: which of the following is not one of the cranial bones
definition: which of the following is not one of the cranial bones

Upper jaw bone. Largest bone of the face. Forms the hard palate, floor of orbit, and upper teeth socket. Paired bones that fuse in the midline. Fractures from facial trauma. Maxillary sinuses are the largest paranasal sinuses.

term: the bone that is part of the shoulder girdle and is between the sternum and the
definition: the bone that is part of the shoulder girdle and is between the sternum and the scapula is the

Collarbone. Connects sternum (breastbone) to scapula (shoulder blade). Most commonly fractured bone in the body (fall on outstretched hand or direct blow). "S"-shaped bone. Landmark for subclavian central line insertion.

term: the type of joint motion that allows movement away from the midline of the body,
definition: the type of joint motion that allows movement away from the midline of the body, such as moving the arms straight out to the side, is

Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body. Opposite: adduction (toward midline). Example: raising arm to the side = shoulder abduction. Hip abductors prevent Trendelenburg gait. Prefix: ab- = away from.

definition: the portion of the brain that aids in the coordination of voluntary muscle action is the

"Little brain" at the back of the skull. Controls coordination, balance, and fine motor movements. Damage: ataxia (poor coordination), dysmetria, intention tremor. NOT responsible for consciousness (that's the cerebral cortex). Cerebrum = higher functions; cerebellum = coordination.

term: a structure found in the inner ear is the
definition: a structure found in the inner ear is the

Snail-shaped structure in the inner ear responsible for hearing (converting sound vibrations to nerve impulses). Contains hair cells on the basilar membrane. Damage to cochlear hair cells causes sensorineural hearing loss (permanent). Connected to auditory nerve (CN VIII).

term: the cluster of cells called the islets of Langerhans are located in the
definition: the cluster of cells called the islets of Langerhans are located in the

Dual-function gland. Endocrine: islets of Langerhans — beta cells secrete insulin (lowers glucose), alpha cells secrete glucagon (raises glucose). Exocrine: secretes digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, protease) into duodenum. Pancreatitis: inflammation, severe epigastric pain radiating to back.

term: a goiter is associated with abnormal function of the
definition: a goiter is associated with abnormal function of the

Butterfly-shaped gland in the neck. Produces T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine) — regulate metabolism. Also produces calcitonin (lowers calcium). Hypothyroidism: low hormone, weight gain, fatigue, cold intolerance. Hyperthyroidism: high hormone, weight loss, tachycardia. TSH from pituitary controls thyroid.

term: the area of the heart that initiates a heartbeat is the
definition: the area of the heart that initiates a heartbeat is the

Natural pacemaker of the heart. Located in the right atrium. Generates electrical impulses at 60–100 bpm. Signal travels: SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibers → ventricular contraction. Sinus rhythm = normal heart rhythm originating from SA node.
A heart disease that is present at birth

First segment of the aorta, rising from the left ventricle. Gives rise to right and left coronary arteries (heart's own blood supply). Continues as aortic arch, then descending aorta. Aortic stenosis (valve narrowing) affects blood leaving ascending aorta.

Central lower abdominal region below the umbilical region. Contains: urinary bladder, uterus (females), sigmoid colon. Pain here may indicate UTI, bladder infection, or reproductive organ issues.

Group of 4 muscles on the anterior thigh: rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius. Function: extend the knee, flex the hip. Vastus lateralis = IM injection site for infants/toddlers. "Quad" = 4.

term: the artery located in the upper arm is the

Main artery of the upper arm. Used for blood pressure measurement (stethoscope placed over brachial artery in antecubital fossa). Palpated on medial aspect of arm. Emergency BP site. Brachial pulse used in infant CPR.

Any substance (protein, polysaccharide, toxin) that triggers an immune response. Foreign antigens stimulate antibody production by B lymphocytes. ABO blood type antigens on RBC surface determine blood type. Antigen-antibody reaction = basis of blood typing, allergy, and infection response.

Slender lateral bone of the lower leg. Does NOT bear significant weight (tibia does). Important as lateral ankle stability. "Fibula fracture" common in ankle sprains. Fibula is the thinner of the two lower leg bones. Fib- = fiber, small.

term: the small pouch that is the first part of the large intestine is the

Blind pouch at the start of the large intestine, in the right iliac fossa (RIF). Appendix attaches to the cecum. Ileocecal valve connects ileum to cecum. Appendicitis: inflammation of appendix (pain begins periumbilical → migrates to RIF/McBurney's point).

Excessive posterior curvature of the thoracic spine — "hunchback." Common in elderly osteoporosis. Distinguished from scoliosis (lateral curve) and lordosis (excessive lumbar curve/"swayback"). Can restrict breathing if severe. Seen from the side.

Fibrous structural protein that forms hair, nails, and the outer layer (stratum corneum) of skin. Makes skin waterproof and protective. Keratinocytes are the main cells of the epidermis. Nails = hardened keratin plates protecting the fingertips.

term: what is the most common condition of the eye associated with aging
definition: what is the most common condition of the eye associated with aging

Age-related farsightedness — loss of near vision due to lens stiffening (typically after age 40). Cannot focus on close objects. Treated with reading glasses or bifocals. Distinguished from hyperopia (structural farsightedness from birth) and myopia (nearsightedness).

term: what structure is at the neck of the bladder and surrounds the urethra
definition: what structure is at the neck of the bladder and surrounds the urethra

Walnut-sized gland in males, surrounds the urethra below the bladder. Produces fluid that nourishes and transports sperm. BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia): common enlargement in older men → urinary retention, frequency. PSA test screens for prostate cancer. Digital rectal exam (DRE) for assessment.

Two small glands located on either side of the vaginal opening. Secrete mucus to lubricate the vagina. Bartholin cyst/abscess: duct blockage causing painful swelling, treated with warm soaks or incision and drainage.

definition: the master gland of the body is the

Anterior: GH (growth), TSH (thyroid-stimulating), ACTH (adrenal-stimulating), FSH (follicle-stimulating), LH (luteinizing), prolactin (milk production). Posterior: ADH/vasopressin (water retention), oxytocin (uterine contraction, bonding). Controlled by hypothalamus via releasing hormones.

term: a fracture of the radius characterized by bending of the bone with the skin left
definition: a fracture of the radius characterized by bending of the bone with the skin left intact is referred to as

Greenstick: incomplete fracture where bone bends and cracks (does not break all the way through) — common in children whose bones are more flexible. Closed (simple): bone broken but skin intact. Open (compound): bone pierces skin — infection risk. Comminuted: bone shattered into fragments.

term: spermatozoa normally fertilize the female ovum in the
definition: spermatozoa normally fertilize the female ovum in the

Two tubes connecting ovaries to uterus (~4 inches each). Fimbriae sweep egg from ovary into tube after ovulation. Fertilization normally occurs in the ampulla of the fallopian tube. Ectopic pregnancy most common here. Salpingitis = infection (often PID from STI).

term: the glomerulus is a cluster of blood capillaries found in the
definition: the glomerulus is a cluster of blood capillaries found in the

Functional filtering unit of the kidney (~1 million per kidney). Components: glomerulus (filtration), Bowman's capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, collecting duct. Produces urine by filtration, reabsorption, and secretion. GFR measures kidney function.
The disorder characterized by uncontrollable episodes of falling asleep

term: the regulation of body temperature is controlled by the
definition: the regulation of body temperature is controlled by the

Region of the brain controlling the pituitary gland via releasing hormones. Regulates: body temperature (thermoregulation), hunger, thirst, sleep, circadian rhythm, and autonomic functions. Links nervous and endocrine systems. Controls "fight or flight" via sympathetic nervous system.

term: Which of the following substances is NOT a neurotransmitter?
definition: Which of the following substances is NOT a neurotransmitter?

Mineralocorticoid hormone produced by adrenal cortex. Increases sodium (and water) reabsorption and potassium excretion by kidneys → raises blood pressure. Regulated by renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS). Hyperaldosteronism (Conn's syndrome): hypertension + hypokalemia.

definition: A blood cell that carries oxygen and has no nucleus is a(n):

Red blood cell. Biconcave disc, no nucleus, lives ~120 days. Contains hemoglobin (Hgb) which carries oxygen. Normal count: males 4.5–5.5 million/µL; females 4.0–5.0 million/µL. Produced in red bone marrow. Destroyed by spleen/liver. Anemia = low RBC/Hgb.

term: during respiration exhaled air contains primarily
definition: during respiration exhaled air contains primarily

Waste gas produced by cellular metabolism. Transported in blood three ways: (1) dissolved in plasma (~7%), (2) bound to hemoglobin as carbaminohemoglobin (~23%), (3) as bicarbonate ions HCO3- (~70%). Exhaled by lungs. Elevated CO2 = hypercapnia; decreased = hypocapnia.

term: a condition causing a back flow of stomach acid through an incompetent esophagea
definition: a condition causing a back flow of stomach acid through an incompetent esophageal sphincter is called

Chronic condition where stomach acid flows back (reflux) into the esophagus, causing heartburn, regurgitation, and esophageal damage. Treated with lifestyle changes, antacids, H2 blockers (Zantac), or PPIs (omeprazole). Complications: Barrett's esophagus, esophageal cancer.

CC, history, exam, detail of the problems, drugs and dosage, assessment, return visit or referral.
